title: What 'Building Stronger Businesses' Actually Means
excerpt: A framework for creating businesses that last.
content:
I often say I'm focused on "building stronger businesses." But what does that actually mean? Here's my working definition.

Strength vs. Scale

A strong business isn't necessarily a large one. Strength is about resilience — the ability to survive and thrive through changing conditions. It's about:

  • Unit economics that work — Every transaction should be profitable on its own
  • Multiple revenue streams — Diversification protects against single points of failure
  • Customer relationships, not transactions — Repeat business is more valuable than one-time sales
  • Low fixed costs — Flexibility to scale up or down as conditions require

The Long Game

I'm not interested in building businesses to flip. I'm interested in building businesses that generate sustainable returns for decades. This requires different thinking:

  • Prioritize cash flow over growth metrics
  • Build real assets, not just user bases
  • Focus on markets with genuine demand, not manufactured hype

Why This Matters

In a world obsessed with unicorns and blitzscaling, I think there's enormous opportunity in boring, profitable businesses that solve real problems. They may not make headlines, but they make money — and they last.

title: My Investment Thesis: Water & Energy Freedom
excerpt: Why I'm focused on the intersection of environmental infrastructure and market mechanisms.
content:
The next decade will be defined by how we manage water and energy resources. I've spent the last several years thinking deeply about this space, and I want to share my framework for evaluating opportunities.

The Core Belief

Water and energy are fundamental. They underpin every economic activity, yet we've historically underpriced and mismanaged both. The correction is coming, and it will create enormous opportunities for those positioned correctly.

What I Look For

When evaluating companies and projects in this space, I focus on:

  1. Market mechanisms — Solutions that create or improve markets for natural resources
  2. Measurability — Technologies that enable better tracking and verification
  3. Scalability — Approaches that can expand without proportional cost increases
  4. Regulatory alignment — Businesses that work with, not against, the policy direction

The Opportunity

Environmental markets are still in their infancy. Water quality trading, carbon markets, and renewable energy certificates are just the beginning. The infrastructure layer — the pipes, sensors, software, and marketplaces — represents the real opportunity.

I'll be sharing more specific insights and case studies in future posts.

title: Hello World
excerpt: A fresh start for my digital home.
content:
Welcome to my new site. I'm rebuilding this from the ground up — simpler, faster, and fully under my control.

This space will be home to my thoughts on building businesses, investing in critical infrastructure, and the intersection of environmental markets and technology.

Why the rebuild?

I wanted a site that:

  • Loads instantly — No bloated frameworks or unnecessary scripts
  • I fully own — Content lives in version control, not a proprietary platform
  • Costs nothing to run — Static hosting is effectively free at this scale

More thoughts coming soon.
